Ref: {{ref}}
ESOP Cost Reference Card
Three-Framework Cost Analysis for {{companyName}}
Company
{{companyName}}
Reference
{{ref}}
Date
{{generatedDate}}
Status
Preliminary
§ 01How to Read This Card

ESOP costs are often presented as a single number, which obscures their true nature. This card uses three frameworks to give a complete picture:

Framework 1: What you pay at closing (one-time transaction costs).
Framework 2: What you pay every year (ongoing stewardship costs).
Framework 3: What it costs over five years (total cost of ownership).
§ 02Framework 1: Transaction Costs (One-Time)

These are the costs incurred to execute the ESOP transaction. They are paid once, at or around closing.

CategoryEstimated Amount
Transaction Costs (trustee, ERISA counsel, valuation, QofE, Forhemit, other){{transactionCosts}}
Total One-Time Transaction Costs{{transactionCosts}}

These costs are typically capitalized into the deal or paid from company operating funds. They are tax-deductible business expenses.

§ 03Framework 2: Annual Stewardship Costs (Ongoing)

After closing, the ESOP requires ongoing professional support to remain compliant and well-managed.

CategoryAnnual Estimate
Annual Stewardship (Forhemit post-close support, trustee oversight, valuation, compliance){{annualStewardship}}
Total Annual Ongoing Costs{{annualStewardship}}

Annual stewardship costs cover the ESOP trustee, annual valuation updates, ERISA compliance, Form 5500 filing support, and Forhemit's operational stewardship. These are recurring operating expenses.

§ 04Framework 3: Total Cost of Ownership (5-Year View)

The total cost of ownership over five years combines one-time transaction costs with five years of annual stewardship:

ComponentAmount
One-Time Transaction Costs{{transactionCosts}}
Annual Stewardship × 5 Years{{annualStewardship}} / yr
Five-Year Total Cost of Ownership{{fiveYearTotal}}
§ 05Comparison to Traditional M&A Costs

For context, the table below compares ESOP costs to a typical third-party sale:

Cost ElementTraditional M&A
Typical Advisory & Closing Costs{{maComparison}}
Key difference: In a third-party sale, the seller typically bears a 4–6% sell-side broker commission plus legal and accounting fees. In an ESOP sale, the company pays all fees as business expenses, and there is no sell-side broker commission. Additionally, §1042 tax deferral can offset many multiples of the transaction cost.
§ 06Important Notes
1.All figures are estimates. Final costs depend on company size, deal complexity, and advisor selection. Detailed quotes will be provided during the engagement process.
2.Tax deductibility. Transaction costs and annual stewardship costs are generally deductible as ordinary business expenses. The seller's CPA should confirm applicability.
3.§1042 offset. The tax savings from §1042 deferral frequently exceed the total transaction cost, making the ESOP economically compelling on a net basis even when gross costs are comparable to M&A.
—Forhemit Transition Stewardship
Forhemit Transition Stewardship
California Public Benefit Corporation
Authorized Representative
